channel.title: Laney Choboy Sports Blog
channel.description: A website about sports and data

item.title: Good Teams Win Late
item.dc:creator: Laney Choboy
item.description:
College football seasons don’t always unfold the way that they began. Some teams begin with a strong performance and sustain that level while others may improve or decline throughout the season. Initial outcomes can influence expectations, yet it is not always evident if those early games forecast long-term season success.

To start, it is beneficial to analyze the performance of teams in the early part of the season, in comparison to their performance throughout the entirety of the season. If initial success is indeed significant, teams that begin strongly ought to sustain that level of performance over time.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)

football_strong <- read_csv("footballlogs25.csv")

football_margins <- football_strong |>
  mutate(
    point_margin = team_pts - opponent_pts,
    start_period = if_else(game_number <= 3, "First 3 Games", "Full Season")
  )

team_summary <- football_margins |>
  group_by(team) |>
  summarise(
    first3_margin = mean(point_margin[game_number <= 3], na.rm = TRUE),
    season_margin = mean(point_margin, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

slope_data <- team_summary |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(first3_margin, season_margin),
    names_to = "period",
    values_to = "point_margin"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    season = if_else(period == "first3_margin", 2024, 2025)
  )
big <- slope_data |>
  filter(team %in% c(
    "Nebraska",
    "Indiana",
    "Ohio State",
    "Oregon",
    "Georgia",
    "Texas Tech",
    "Miami (FL)",
    "Iowa",
    "Michigan",
    "Texas",
    "Purdue",
    "UCLA",
    "Oklahoma State",
    "Charlotte",
    "Massachusetts"
  ))

nu <- big |> filter(team == "Nebraska")
oh <- big |> filter(team == "Ohio State")
id <- big |> filter(team == "Indiana")
ggplot() + 
  geom_line(data=big, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=big, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data=nu, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="red") +
  geom_point(data=nu, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="red") +
  geom_line(data=oh, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="black") +
  geom_point(data=oh, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="black") +
  geom_line(data=id, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="blue") +
  geom_point(data=id, aes(x=season, y=point_margin, group=team), color="blue") +
  geom_text(data=nu |> filter(season == max(season)), aes(x=season + .2, y=point_margin+.1, group=team, label=team)) + 
  geom_text(data=nu |> filter(season == min(season)), aes(x=season - .2, y=point_margin, group=team, label=team)) + 
  geom_text(data=id |> filter(season == max(season)), aes(x=season + .2, y=point_margin+.1, group=team, label=team)) + 
  geom_text(data=id |> filter(season == min(season)), aes(x=season - .2, y=point_margin, group=team, label=team)) + 
  geom_text(data=oh |> filter(season == max(season)), aes(x=season + .2, y=point_margin+.1, group=team, label=team)) + 
  geom_text(data=oh |> filter(season == min(season)), aes(x=season - .2, y=point_margin, group=team, label=team)) + 
  scale_x_continuous(
    breaks=c(2024, 2025), 
    labels=c("First 3 Games", "Full Season"),
    limits=c(2023.5,2025.5)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Starting fast does not always mean finishing strong",
    subtitle = "Nebraska improved from its first three games, while stronger teams stayed dominant throughout the season.",
    x = "",
    y = "Average point margin",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | Credit: Laney Choboy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Across teams like Nebraska, Ohio State, and Indiana football distinct patterns can be observed. Teams that start the season strongly tend to maintain their performance, yet there are also those teams that either enhance their play as the season progresses or decline after a promising beginning.

This variability indicates that early-season performance influences outcomes, but it does not completely dictate how the remainder of the season unfolds. Certain teams adapt, improve or encounter more competition as the year advances.

Analyzing the overall trends among teams provides insight into whether early season performance is genuinely linked to success throughout the season.

Code 
scatter_teams <- football_strong |>
  filter(team %in% c(
    "Nebraska",
    "Indiana",
    "Ohio State",
    "Oregon",
    "Georgia",
    "Texas Tech",
    "Miami (FL)",
    "Iowa",
    "Michigan",
    "Texas",
    "Purdue",
    "UCLA",
    "Oklahoma State",
    "Charlotte",
    "Massachusetts"
  )) |>
  mutate(
    point_margin = team_pts - opponent_pts,
    won_game = team_pts > opponent_pts
  )

team_scatter <- scatter_teams |>
  group_by(team) |>
  summarise(
    first3_margin = mean(point_margin[game_number <= 3], na.rm = TRUE),
    season_win_pct = mean(won_game, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

losers <- team_scatter |> filter(season_win_pct > .5)

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=team_scatter, aes(x=first3_margin, y=season_win_pct), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=losers, aes(x=first3_margin, y=season_win_pct), color="red") +  
  geom_text_repel(data=losers, aes(x=first3_margin, y=season_win_pct, label=team)) +  
  labs(
    title = "Fast starts can point toward better seasons",
    subtitle = "Among 15 teams chosen at random, point margins in the first 3 games generally connect to higher win percentages.",
    x = "Average point margin in first 3 games",
    y = "Season win percentage",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | Credit: Laney Choboy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

There is a noticeable relationship between point margins in the early season and overall success. Teams that perform better in their first few games tend to win more across the season. The general trend suggests that early success is often a good indicator of future performance.

However the relationship is not perfect. Some teams can recover from a slow start while others struggle to maintain their early success. This shows that while winning the first few games matters, it is only one piece to a bigger picture.

To better understand what is truly driving these results, it helps to look at how teams are distributed overall. This can show whether early success is as big of a factor as some may think. Do stronger teams simply perform well regardless of how they start their season?

Code 
set.seed(1234)

activeplayers <- football_strong |>
  mutate(
    point_margin = team_pts - opponent_pts,
    won_game = team_pts > opponent_pts
  ) |>
  filter(team %in% c(
    "Nebraska",
    "Indiana",
    "Ohio State",
    "Oregon",
    "Georgia",
    "Texas Tech",
    "Miami (FL)",
    "Iowa",
    "Michigan",
    "Texas",
    "Purdue",
    "UCLA",
    "Oklahoma State",
    "Charlotte",
    "Massachusetts"
  )) |>
  group_by(team) |>
  summarise(
    win_pct = mean(won_game, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  filter(win_pct >= 0)
nu <- activeplayers |> 
  filter(team == "Nebraska") 

oh <- activeplayers |> 
  filter(team == "Ohio State")

id <- activeplayers |> 
  filter(team == "Indiana")

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(
    data=activeplayers, 
    aes(x=reorder(team,win_pct), weight=win_pct), 
    fill="lightgrey"
  ) +
   geom_bar(
    data=nu, 
    aes(x = reorder(team, win_pct), weight = win_pct),
    fill="#d00000"
   ) +
     geom_bar(
    data=oh, 
    aes(x = reorder(team, win_pct), weight = win_pct),
    fill="#BA0C2F"
     ) +
  geom_bar(
    data=id, 
    aes(x = reorder(team, win_pct), weight = win_pct),
    fill="#990000"
     ) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Win Percentage Varies Across Power Programs",
    subtitle = "Nebraska, Ohio State and Indiana show how performance stacks up against a wider group of teams",
    x = "",
    y = "Win Percentage",
    caption = "Source: Sports Reference | Credit: Laney Choboy"
  ) +
theme_minimal() +
theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 15, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Stronger teams tend to cluster toward higher overall performance across the season similar to the highest level volleyball teams and winning first sets. Programs like Ohio State consistently perform at a high level, while others show more variation. Nebraska and Indiana are more of the middle ground where results can shift depending on how the season develops. This suggests that early success and overall performance are connected but not identical. Better teams tend to start strong but Nebraska has recently started strong and ended poorly. Overall consistency and team quality are more important.

Fast starts at the beginning of the season matter in college football, but they do not define the entire outcome. They provide an early advantage, but maintaining that advantage depends on consistency over the full season.

In the end, early-season performance helps shape results, but overall team quality plays the biggest role in determining success.

item.title: How Much Does The First Set Affect The Outcome Of The Match
item.dc:creator: Laney Choboy
item.description:
How much does the first set matter in volleyball? There’s a known saying that says “set the tone early”. Is that true or is that something that just sounds good?

It feels like the team that wins the first set usually wins the match but is that a real statistic or is that something we think we see?

Take a look at the top teams in the country for 2025 and see what happens when they win the first set versus what happens if they lose the first set.

To answer that question, here is the data from some of the top programs in NCAA Division 1 volleyball. Teams like Nebraska, Wisconsin, Texas and Pittsburgh etc.

First, look at how often teams win matches after taking the first set.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)

set_scores <- read_csv("set_scores.csv")

goodteams <- c("Nebraska", "Penn St.", "Pittsburgh", "Kentucky", "Wisconsin", "Texas A&M", "Texas", "Stanford", "Purdue", "Louisville", "Creighton", "SMU", "Southern California", "Arizona State")

good_set_score <- set_scores |> filter(team1 %in% goodteams) 

total_wins <- good_set_score |>
  filter(team1_sets_won == 3) |> 
  group_by(team1) |> 
  summarize(
    wins = n()
  )

set1wins <- good_set_score |> 
  filter(s1_t1 > s1_t2) |> 
  group_by(team1) |> 
  summarize(
    set_1_wins = n()
  )

wins <- total_wins |> inner_join(set1wins)

wins <- good_set_score |>
  group_by(team1) |> 
  summarise(
    set1wins = sum(s1_t1 > s1_t2), 
    matchwins = sum(team1_sets_won ==3))

ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=wins, 
    aes(
      y=team1, yend=team1,
      x=set1wins, xend=matchwins)
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=wins,
    aes(
      y=team1,
      x=set1wins,
      color="Set 1"
    )
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=wins,
    aes(
      y=team1,
      x=matchwins,
      color="Win"
    )
  ) + 
  scale_color_manual(name="Wins", values=c("blue", "green")) +
  labs(
    title = "Top Teams Usually Win Set 1 ... and the Match",
    subtitle = "This compares how often each team won the opening set versus how many total matches it won.",
    x = "Number of wins",
    y = "Team",
    caption = "Source: set_scores.csv | Chart by Laney Choboy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Across the top programs in the country, winning the first set lines up closely with winning matches overall. Teams that constantly take set 1 also tend to have the most wins.

A few schools stand out here. Nebraska stands out a lot in 2025 not just because they win a lot of matches, but because they are consistently converting strong starts into winning matches.

This is not saying that if you lose the first set you lose the game and we can see that from this dumbbell chart but it is suggesting that the first set is setting the direction for everything that follows.

Now dig deeper.

Code 
set1_summary <- good_set_score |> 
  mutate(
    won_set1 = s1_t1 > s1_t2,
    won_match = team1_sets_won == 3
  ) |> 
  group_by(won_set1) |> 
  summarise(
    win_pct = mean(won_match)
  )

set1 <- good_set_score |>
  filter(s1_t1 > s1_t2) |>
  group_by(team1) |>
  tally() |>
  ungroup() |>
  rename(Set1Wins = n)

noset1 <- good_set_score |>
  filter(s1_t1 < s1_t2) |> 
  group_by(team1) |>
  tally() |>
  ungroup() |>
  rename(Set1Losses = n)

nu <- set1 |> 
  filter(team1 == "Nebraska")

ggplot() + 
  geom_bar(
    data=set1, 
    aes(x = reorder(team1, Set1Wins), weight = Set1Wins),
    fill="grey"
  ) + 
  geom_bar(
    data=nu, 
    aes(x = reorder(team1, Set1Wins), weight = Set1Wins),
    fill="red"
  ) + 
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Winning Set 1 Matters, Especially for Nebraska",
    subtitle = "Teams that win the first set tend to win more matches; Nebraska stands out among top programs.",
    x = "Team",
    y = "Number of Matches Won After Taking Set 1",
    caption = "Source: NCAA match data | Chart by Laney Choboy"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 20, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

It’s one thing to say when teams win first sets they win the match, but how closely do those numbers actually correlate?

This compares the numbers side by side.

This chart shows each team’s set 1 wins compared directly towards their total match wins.

This chart shows a similar stat to the first one. The more often they win the first set, the more matches they win.

The gap between first set wins and total match wins is typically pretty small which states that if a team starts strong they usually finish strong as well.

There aren’t many teams that can consistently lose the first set and still come out on top.

At this point. It’s safe to say, winning the first set is heavily tied to winning the match. That brings up another question.

Are these teams just better overall?

To answer that, you can look at two stats in particular. Hitting percentage and opponent hitting percentage.

Code 
teamstats <- read_csv("https://mattwaite.github.io/sportsdatafiles/ncaa_womens_volleyball_teams_2025.csv")

goodteams <- c(
  "Nebraska", "Penn St.", "Pittsburgh", "Kentucky", "Wisconsin",
  "Texas A&M", "Texas", "Stanford", "Purdue", "Louisville",
  "Creighton", "SMU", "Southern California", "Arizona State"
)

goodteamstats <- teamstats |> 
  filter(team %in% goodteams) |> 
  select(team, hit_pct, opponent_hit_pct)

goodteamstats |> 
  arrange(desc(hit_pct)) |> 
  gt() |> 
  cols_label(
    hit_pct = "Hitting Percentage",
    opponent_hit_pct = "Opponent Hitting Percentage"
  ) |> 
  tab_header(
    title = "Are these teams just really good or does the first set matter?",
    subtitle = "Yes the first set matters but the stats also matter. Here is a few stats to show that these teams are also just really good."
  ) |>  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", weight = "bold", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("title")
  ) |>  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(color = "black", align = "left"),
    locations = cells_title("subtitle")
  ) |> 
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("By: Laney Choboy  |  **Source:** NCAA")
  ) |> 
  tab_style(
    locations = cells_column_labels(columns = everything()),
    style = list(
      cell_borders(sides = "bottom", weight = px(3)),
      cell_text(weight = "bold", size = 12)
    )
  ) |> 
  opt_row_striping() |> 
  opt_table_lines("none") |> 
  tab_style(
    style = list(
      cell_fill(color = "red"),
      cell_text(color = "white")
    ),
    locations = cells_body(
      rows = team == "Nebraska"
    ) 
  )
Are these teams just really good or does the first set matter?
Yes the first set matters but the stats also matter. Here is a few stats to show that these teams are also just really good.
team Hitting Percentage Opponent Hitting Percentage
Nebraska 0.351 0.129
Wisconsin 0.327 0.187
Texas 0.315 0.199
SMU 0.315 0.182
Pittsburgh 0.312 0.174
Stanford 0.299 0.188
Texas A&M 0.297 0.186
Purdue 0.290 0.220
Kentucky 0.289 0.190
Creighton 0.288 0.183
Louisville 0.270 0.173
Southern California 0.259 0.184
Penn St. 0.250 0.238
By: Laney Choboy | Source: NCAA

This chart tells something important. The top teams in the country aren’t just winning because they can take the first set, they are also winning because they are statistically better than their opponent.

The higher a team’s hitting percentage is and the lower their opponent hitting percentage is, shows that these teams can control a match the whole time, not just in the beginning.

Winning the first set definitely matters more than people would think but it’s not just set 1 that determines the match, it’s that teams good enough to win the match, usually are good enough to win the first set.

While winning the first set doesn’t automatically mean you’ve won the game, it is a statistically strong indicator that you have what it takes to win the match.

item.title: Post With Code
item.dc:creator: Harlow Malloc
item.description:
This is a post with executable code.

1 + 1
[1] 2

item.title: Welcome To My Blog
item.dc:creator: Tristan O'Malley
item.description:
This is the first post in a Quarto blog. Welcome!

Since this post doesn’t specify an explicit image, the first image in the post will be used in the listing page of posts.
